species: Trumpeter Swan + Sandhill Crane -- trumpet + bugle dual layer, delta, winter
scientific_name: Cygnus buccinator (Trumpeter Swan, Family Anatidae) + Antigone canadensis (Sandhill Crane, Family Gruidae)
sound_type: Trumpet + bugle dual layer. Trumpeter Swan: fundamental 250-350 Hz, harmonics 500-700 Hz, smooth resonant character, 90-100 dB at source, carries several miles, 1.5-3 sec call duration. Sandhill Crane: fundamental 250-350 Hz, rattling harmonics 300-1200 Hz, 95-105 dB at source, carries 2-3 miles, 1-4 sec per call. Combined dual layer: 250-1200 Hz, 95-110 dB at congregation site. Both species calling simultaneously at Skagit delta winter congregation. Duration: active throughout daylight hours at congregation, peak calling during flock movement (morning departure, afternoon return).
ecosystem: Delta, estuarine and agricultural delta habitats. Skagit River delta (Skagit County WA): Samish Flats, Conway, Mount Vernon area flooded agricultural fields. Fraser River delta (BC): Boundary Bay, Reifel Migratory Bird Sanctuary. Both species use flooded agricultural fields and shallow wetlands. Concurrent species at Skagit: Tundra Swan, Snow Geese, Dunlin, Northern Harrier.
season: Winter (Trumpeter Swan: November-March). Sandhill Crane: migration (spring/fall) with some winter presence in mild Pacific Northwest years. Peak dual-species overlap: November-February at Skagit and Fraser deltas.
family: Multi-species. Family Anatidae: Cygnus buccinator (Trumpeter Swan). Family Gruidae: Antigone canadensis (Sandhill Crane, subspecies A. c. tabida Greater Sandhill Crane in western WA).
milestone: FIRST GRUIDAE in SPS engine. FIRST ANATIDAE at maximum (Trumpeter Swan = loudest waterfowl, largest North American waterfowl). OLDEST BIRD CALL IN ENGINE: Sandhill Crane fossil record 2.5 million years (Pliocene), morphologically identical to modern birds. Trumpeter Swan near-extinction recovery: fewer than 100 birds (early 20th century) to 63,000+ (2020s). Convergent vocal anatomy: both species independently evolved elongated trachea coiled within sternum as resonating chamber. Dual-layer: smooth trumpet (swan) + rattling bugle (crane) simultaneously = two different acoustic characters from same fundamental frequency range.

coordinates.description: Representative: Skagit River delta / Samish Flats, Skagit County WA. 48.37°N 122.43°W. Primary winter concentration area for Trumpeter Swans; Sandhill Crane migration stopover. Also: Reifel Migratory Bird Sanctuary, Fraser River delta BC (49.12°N 123.20°W).

acoustic_data.trumpeter_swan: Fundamental: 250-350 Hz. Harmonics: 500-700 Hz. Spectral character: smooth, bell-like, well-defined harmonics (French horn / tuba quality). Amplitude: 90-100 dB at source. Carrying distance: several miles in open delta. Tracheal anatomy: coiled within sternal groove, up to 1.5m total. Call duration: 1.5-3 seconds. Species name buccinator = Latin 'trumpeter'.
acoustic_data.sandhill_crane: Fundamental: 250-350 Hz. Harmonics: 300-1200 Hz (rattling quality from syrinx soft tissue vibration). Spectral character: rattling, complex, penetrating. Amplitude: 95-105 dB at source. Carrying distance: 2-3 miles. Tracheal anatomy: 1.2-1.5m coiled within sternum. Call duration: 1-4 seconds, often in rolling sequences. Fossil record: 2.5 million years (Pliocene, Ashfall Fossil Beds Nebraska).
acoustic_data.combined_dual_layer: 250-1200 Hz. Two fundamentals at same frequency (250-350 Hz) but different spectral envelopes (swan: smooth; crane: rattling). Distinguishable by ear at 100-200m. Combined amplitude at congregation: 95-110 dB. Peak calling: morning departure and afternoon return of flocks.
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8 through dual-species iconic voice. Neither species alone reaches E=8 (each approximately E=7-8 at congregation scale). Both simultaneously: E=8 through combined cultural and acoustic significance. Two of North America's most acoustically powerful large birds calling simultaneously at a documented Pacific Northwest winter congregation site.

nodes.title: FIRST GRUIDAE -- Sandhill Crane Enters Engine at E=8
nodes.description: Family Gruidae (cranes) makes its first appearance at degree 330. The Sandhill Crane brings the most ancient acoustic record in the engine: 2.5 million years of fossil continuity. The crane's call is essentially unchanged from the Pliocene.

nodes.title: Oldest Bird Call in Engine -- 2.5 Million Year Fossil Record
nodes.description: Sandhill Crane (Antigone canadensis) fossil bones morphologically identical to modern birds found in Pliocene deposits (Ashfall Fossil Beds, Nebraska, and other sites). The call documented at degree 330 is, as closely as can be determined, the same call produced 2.5 million years ago. The engine's most ancient acoustic record.

nodes.title: Convergent Tracheal Anatomy -- Same Solution, Different Sound
nodes.description: Trumpeter Swan and Sandhill Crane independently evolved elongated tracheae coiled within the sternum as resonating chambers. Same anatomy, different acoustic outputs: Swan produces smooth bell-like resonance; Crane produces rattling complex bugle. Convergent evolution in vocal anatomy documented acoustically.

nodes.title: Trumpeter Swan Recovery -- Fewer than 100 to 63,000+
nodes.description: Near-extinct by early 20th century (fewer than 100 in contiguous US due to hunting). Migratory Bird Treaty Act (1918) and subsequent protected area management restored population to 63,000+ by 2020s. One of North America's greatest conservation recoveries. The loudest waterfowl call nearly silenced permanently -- now filling Pacific Northwest deltas in winter.

cross_references.relationship: E=8 + E=8 = E=16. Three great voices at full expression: Geoff Tate (five-octave operatic metal vocal range), Trumpeter Swan (loudest waterfowl 90-100 dB, smooth trumpet), Sandhill Crane (prehistoric bugle 95-105 dB, rattling complex). All three at maximum vocal capability in their respective domains.
E=9 first insect sound to E=8 dual bird call. Consecutive degrees, consecutive milestones. Mass biological emergence (E=9) followed by iconic individual great-voice event (E=8).

First Accipitridae (degree 221) to First Gruidae (degree 330). 109 degrees between bird family firsts. Eagle at E=5; dual bird call at E=8. The engine documents iconic North American birds across the full degree range.
